ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle soup class; no specific spicy ramen with egg subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers ramen noodle soup as a category. No specific spicy variant with egg exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for spicy ramen with egg as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of spicy ramen with egg weighs approximately 500 g, comprising noodles, broth, one soft-boiled egg, and toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle soup, with egg — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for ramen noodle soup (FDC ID 167898) and hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424), adjusted for spicy seasoning
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for spicy ramen with egg as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for ramen noodle soup (FDC ID 167898, SR Legacy) with hard-boiled egg contribution (FDC ID 173424) and a spicy seasoning adjustment. The egg contributes approximately 10–12 g per 100 g of final dish weight. Energy and macros are weighted averages across broth, noodles, egg, and toppings at a 500 g serving size. Sodium is elevated due to broth and soy-based seasoning. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, ramen type — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to spicy ramen with egg
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy ramen with egg as a composite dish. GI of 55 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for ramen-type noodles (GI approximately 52–58, medium category). The egg and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 57.5 g) / 100 ≈ 31.6. Available carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 11.5 g/100 g × 500 g = 57.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content from broth and soy-based seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit spicy ramen consumption due to approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 2400 mg per standard 500 g bowl), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming only half the broth is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate load from wheat-based ramen noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 500 g bowl of spicy ramen with egg carries a glycemic load of approximately 31.6, which is high; portion control, choosing a smaller serving, and pairing with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from broth, egg yolk, and seasoning oils
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit spicy ramen with egg due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (approximately 0.9 g per 100 g), which together may contribute to elevated blood pressure and unfavourable lipid profiles; opting for a smaller portion, reduced-sodium broth, and limiting frequency of consumption is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy ramen with egg is a Japanese noodle soup dish consisting of wheat-based ramen noodles served in a rich, spicy broth — typically chicken, pork (tonkotsu), or miso-based — seasoned with chili paste, soy sauce, and sesame oil, and topped with a soft-boiled or marinated egg (ajitsuke tamago), nori seaweed, spring onions, bamboo shoots, and sometimes corn or bean sprouts. The dish is characterised by its bold, umami-rich and spicy flavour profile, with chewy noodles and a silky, flavourful broth. The egg adds creaminess and additional protein. Nutritionally, spicy ramen with egg provides moderate energy (approximately 95 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from both the broth and egg, and moderate carbohydrates from the noodles. The primary clinical concern is its very high sodium content, typically 400–550 mg per 100 g, making a standard bowl a significant sodium load for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is medium (approximately 55), driven by the wheat noodles. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its standard form due to animal-based broth and egg. Spicy ramen with egg is widely available across Japan and in Japanese restaurants globally, including Singapore, where it is a popular dining-out option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in spicy ramen with egg?
Is spicy ramen with egg healthy?
How much sodium is in a bowl of spicy ramen?
Is ramen with egg high in protein?
What is the GI of ramen noodles?
Is spicy ramen with egg halal?
What are the ingredients in spicy ramen with egg?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for spicy ramen with egg as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for ramen noodle soup and hard-boiled egg components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for ramen-type noodles. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and variability across restaurant preparations.
notes: 1. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under food_group 'Dairy and eggs' and described it as 'chicken soup'; both have been corrected. The correct food_group is 'Mixed' and the food_subgroup is 'Noodle soup dishes'. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for ramen noodle soup (FDC ID 167898) and hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424), weighted for a 500 g serving. Actual restaurant values may vary. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry for this composite dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for ramen-type noodles; no direct measurement for spicy ramen with egg exists in the approved GI database. 5. Halal status is unconfirmed as pork-based tonkotsu broth is common in ramen; is_halal is set to false by default. 6. The input source (field survey data) is a forbidden source for nutrient data and has not been used for nutritional values. Restaurant metadata (Ramen Matsuri, 7 North Canal Road) is preserved for provenance context only. 7. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_00002700 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for spicy ramen with egg.
